title: FINS3616 Week 8 Updated
body:
FINS3616 INTERNATIONAL BUSINESS FINANCE


                     LECTURE 8
       INTERNATIONAL CORPORATE ISSUES


       Janghoon Shon (University of New South Wales)
 Visit Moodle to complete
the myExperience survey
 FINAL EXAM

Format:            Inspera Exam (Central Final Exam Period – In Person)
Time:              22 August (PROVISIONAL, by the school)
Question types:    19 single choices, 9 numerical responses (fill-in blanks), and 3 short essay questions
Duration:          2 hours
Coverage:          Week 1 – Week 10
% Evaluation:      40%


Essay Material Provided
MOCK EXAM PROVIDED


                                                                                                               3
 PLAN

W1: Introduction to International finance & exchange rates
W2: Description of FX markets & analysis of exchange rate risk
W3: International parity conditions
W4: Exchange rate systems and fundamentals
W5: Exchange rate trading and forecasting
    W6: Flexi Week
W7: Foreign currency derivatives
W8: FX risk hedging and management within multinationals
W9: International corporate issues
W10: International financing: cross-listing and Eurobonds

                                                                    4
 TODAY

1. The Cost of Capital for Foreign Investment


2. Project vs. Parent value


3. The Value of the Multinational Financial System




                                                        5
 THE COST OF CAPITAL FOR FOREIGN INVESTMENT
 THE COST OF CAPITAL FOR FOREIGN INVESTMENT

The Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC)
•   Foreign investment is often financed by a combination of equity and debt, we will use the weighted average cost of
    capital (WACC).
•   The weighted average of all the constituent components of capital (equity, debt, preferred stocks et al.) in the
    capital structure:
                                                  𝑾𝑨𝑪𝑪 = 𝒘𝒆 𝒌𝒆 + 𝒘𝒅 𝒌𝒅

                                                      The weighted average cost of capital (WACC) is an estimate of how
    Where:
                                                      much it will cost the MNC to raise additional capital to finance its
    𝑘𝑒 is the cost of equity,                         investment.
    𝑘𝑑 is the after-tax cost of debt,
    𝑤 is the relevant proportion of capital structure coming from equity (𝑤𝑒) and from debt (𝑤𝑑) for this project.



                                                                                                                          7
 THE COST OF CAPITAL FOR FOREIGN INVESTMENT

The Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC)
•   Typically, MNCs have target capital structures specific to each project. This gives us an indication of the respective
    weights of the permanent sources of financing.
•   If there is no target capital structure for the project, then we obtain the weightings that reflect the capital
    structure of the firm itself.
•   The cost of equity and cost of debt should reflect the required return for this project –project-specific!
•   The weighting should generally reflect the future capital structure of the project or subsidiary:
     •   Often projects (or subsidiaries or assets) will rely on a similar capital structure to the parent.
     •   Some projects might need a different capital structure (i.e., if the project can only support a certain level of
         debt), in which case project-specific capital structure should be used.


                                                                                                                            8
 THE COST OF CAPITAL FOR FOREIGN INVESTMENT

The Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC)
•   Example
Shell is evaluating the financial viability of a new project in Indonesia. Shell utilizes only two sources of
external financing: long-term debt and common equity. Its long-term debt ratio is 47%.


The CFO of Shell suggests that the project’s capital structure ought to reflect the capital structure of Shell
itself. Common equity holders require a return of 14.70% and the pre-tax cost of debt is 6%.


What is the WACC, supposing the marginal tax rate is 21%


                       𝑊𝐴𝐶𝐶 = (1−0.47)×14.70% + 0.47×(1−0.21)×6% = 10.0188%
                                                                                                                   9
 THE COST OF CAPITAL FOR FOREIGN INVESTMENT

Cost of equity
•   Common equity holders have only a residual claim on the FCF of the company. As such, they face the highest risk
    of all the providers of external financing of the firm.
•   Cost of equity: the minimum (required) rate of return necessary to compensate investors to buy or hold the
    firm’s stock.
•   Used to value future equity cash flows.




                                                                                                                     10
 THE COST OF CAPITAL FOR FOREIGN INVESTMENT

Cost of equity
Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM) provides a methodology for quantifying market risk and translating that risk into
estimates of expected return on equity.
For an individual asset i, its expected return E(Ri) is

                                                     𝐸 𝑅𝑖 = 𝑅𝑓 + 𝛽𝑖 𝐸(𝑅𝑚 − 𝑅𝑓 ]

      where
              Rf is the risk-free rate, i.e. the return required on the time value of money.
              𝛽𝑖 is the beta of asset i, which measure how the asset’s return comoves with the market
              𝐸(𝑅𝑚 ) is the expected return on the market portfolio
              𝐸(𝑅𝑚 ) − 𝑅𝑓 is the premium demanded by investors for investing in the market portfolio.


                                                                                                                          11
 THE COST OF CAPITAL FOR FOREIGN INVESTMENT

Cost of equity
•   However, many projects may not have enough historical data, and need to construct a proxy from the betas of comparable firms
    or projects that share similar risk characteristics.


•   Issues to consider when calculating the beta for a foreign investment:
      •   1. Should we use domestic or foreign (local) comparable firms as proxy?
           •       Ideally use local firms (i.e., in the project’s country)
           •       Local firms better reflect the local project’s risk exposure
           •       Obtain multiple proxy firms. They should be in the same industry as the underlying project


               .




                                                                                                                              12
 THE COST OF CAPITAL FOR FOREIGN INVESTMENT

Cost of equity
•   2. Should we use domestic, foreign, or world market portfolio as the benchmark portfolio?
     •   Country-specific risk is diversifiable through international portfolio management. Globalization lead to lower cost of
         capital
     •   Should use the parent’s market portfolio or world market portfolio depending on the parent’s portfolio and belief on
         global market integration
           •       Use world portfolio if believe capital markets are globally integrated
           •       If not, use parent portfolio, also ensure comparability with domestic projects
     •   Using foreign market portfolio may overestimate cost of capital


               .




                                                                                                                                     13
 THE COST OF CAPITAL FOR FOREIGN INVESTMENT

Cost of equity: Hamada equation
•   Beta (proxied beta) includes the effects of debt financing.
•   Different proxy firms have different capital structures from that of the project.


•   General steps for estimating beta of a project:
      •   Calculate comparable firms’ betas relative to the parent’s market.
      •   “Delever” the beta for each comparable firm.                                        𝛽𝐿
      •   Calculate the average all-equity/unlevered beta.                     𝛽𝑈 =
                                                                                                        𝐷
                                                                                        1 + (1 − 𝑇𝐶 )
      •   “Relever” beta using project-specific capital structure                                       𝐸

                                           𝐷
                𝛽𝐿 = 𝛽𝑈 × 1 + (1 − 𝑇𝐶 )
                                           𝐸



                                                                                                               14
 THE COST OF CAPITAL FOR FOREIGN INVESTMENT

Cost of equity: Hamada equation
•   Example:
Suppose DPG wants to finance a project with an outlay of $170 million. The company will target a long-term debt to equity ratio of
1.125. The market value of the firm’s current debt and equity produces a ratio of 0.5 and its sensitivity is set at 0.8.
Assuming DPG faces a marginal corporate tax rate of 30%, what sensitivity will DPG’s common equity holders face on the new
acquisition?
Suppose the risk-free rate is 2.50% and the expected return on the market for this financial year is expected to be 10%.


Calculate the expected return the common equity holders will demand.




                                                                                                                                15
 THE COST OF CAPITAL FOR FOREIGN INVESTMENT

Cost of equity: Hamada equation
•   Example:
                                        𝛽𝐿                    0.8
      •   Step 1           𝛽𝑈 =                     =                      = 0.593
                                                𝐷                    0.5
                                  1 + (1 − 𝑇𝐶 ) 𝐸       1 + (1 − 0.3) 1


                                                   𝐷                         1.125
      •   Step 2           𝛽𝐿 = 𝛽𝑈 × 1 + (1 − 𝑇𝐶 )   = 0.593 × 1 + (1 − 0.3)       = 1.059
                                                   𝐸                           1


      •   Cost of Equity   𝑟𝑒 = 𝑟𝑓 + 𝛽𝑙 [𝑟𝑚 − 𝑟𝑓 ] = 0.025 + 1.0590.10 − 0.025 = 0.104425 = 10.443%



                                                                                                     16
 THE COST OF CAPITAL FOR FOREIGN INVESTMENT

Cost of debt
•   The cost of debt is the return that lenders demand to finance a project of this level of risk.
•   Foreign subsidiaries can borrow either in the parent’s market or the local market.
•   Factors to bear in mind:
      •   Borrowing in the parent’s market cannot lower foreign exchange risk.
      •   Borrowing in the subsidiary’s market may lower foreign exchange risk if the parent is able to net the subsidiary’s revenues
          with the borrowing costs.
      •   After considering exchange rate appreciation/depreciation, in equilibrium, the parent should be indifferent between
          borrowing in the parent’s market or the subsidiary’s market. (Recall the parity conditions)




                                                                                                                                   17
 THE COST OF CAPITAL FOR FOREIGN INVESTMENT

Cost of debt
The after-tax cost of foreign currency debt is affected by
           •   interest rate on the debt (r),
           •   any currency gains or losses (c), and
           •   the effects of taxes (t):


                             𝑪𝒐𝒔𝒕 𝒐𝒇 𝒇𝒐𝒓𝒆𝒊𝒈𝒏 𝒅𝒆𝒃𝒕 𝒊𝒏 𝑫𝒐𝒎𝒆𝒔𝒕𝒊𝒄 𝑪𝒖𝒓𝒓𝒆𝒏𝒄𝒚 = 𝒓 𝟏 + 𝒄 𝟏 − 𝒕 + 𝒄
    Where
           •   𝑟 is the interest rate on the foreign loan
           •   𝑐 is the % change in the value of the nominal value of the currency in which the debt is denominated (i.e., for a €
               debt, the % change in the value of €1)
           •   t is the relevant marginal tax rate for the foreign subsidiary. Interest expense is tax deductible.
                                                                                                                                    18
 THE COST OF CAPITAL FOR FOREIGN INVESTMENT

Cost of debt: Example

Suppose the German Subsidiary of a US MNC borrows €10m for one year at an interest rate of 7%. The current exchange rate is
USD 1.47 = EUR 1. In one year, the exchange rate is USD 1.41 = EUR 1. What was the ex-post cost of the loan?
Approach 1:
     % Change in the EUR: (1.41 – 1.47)/1.47 = - 4.08%, a depreciation in EUR
     Ex post cost of the loan = 7%(1 – 4.08%) - 4.08% = 2.63%
Approach 2:
     At an interest rate of 7%, MNC need to repay €10.7m.
     The loan amount at time 0 is €10m x 𝑒0 = 10m x 1.47 = $14.7m
     The repayment amount in a year is €10.7m x 𝑒1 = 10.7m x 1.41 = $15.087m
     Cost is 15.087/14.7 = 1.0263 → 2.63%
                                                                                                                             19
 THE COST OF CAPITAL FOR FOREIGN INVESTMENT

Cost of debt: Example
Now, suppose the marginal tax of the German subsidiary is 20%.

What was the ex post cost of the loan?



     Ex post after-tax cost of the loan = 7%(1 – 4.08%)(1 - 20%) - 4.08% = 1.29%
   or
     After deducting the tax saving (20%) from the € 0.7m interest, the after-tax interest is € 0.56m = € 0.7m x (1 - 20%)
     The after-tax repayment amount in a year is €10.56m x 1.41 = $14.89m
     After-tax cost is 14.89/14.7 = 1.0129 → 1.29%



                                                                                                                                20
 PROJECT AND PARENT PERSPECTIVES
 PROJECT AND PARENT PERSPECTIVES

There are two ways to value a foreign project:
1) The project’s perspective: Value the project in the foreign currency (the present value of cash flows and
   discount rates in the foreign currency) then convert the value of the project into the parent’s (domestic)
   currency.                                              𝑇                𝑓
                                             𝑃𝑟𝑜𝑗𝑒𝑐𝑡                𝐸[𝐶𝐹𝑡 ]
                                           𝑉0        = 𝑆 𝑑/𝑓 ×   ෍
                                                                   (1 + 𝑖𝑓 )𝑡
                                                                 𝑡=1


2) The parent’s perspective: Convert the cash flows to the parent’s (domestic) currency and value the
   project using domestic discount rates.

                                                          𝑇            𝑓
                                              𝑃𝑎𝑟𝑒𝑛𝑡
                                                         𝐸[𝐶𝐹𝑡 ] × 𝑆 𝑑/𝑓
                                             𝑉0      = ෍
                                                           (1 + 𝑖𝑑 )𝑡
                                                         𝑡=1



                                                                                                               22
 PROJECT AND PARENT PERSPECTIVES

Example:
You work for an Australian firm that is considering a foreign investment in Europe. The investment yields expected
after-tax Euro (EUR) cash flows as follows:




Expected inflation is 4.0% in Australia and 5.0% in Europe. Assume that the international parity conditions hold.
Required returns for projects in this risk class are 10.000% in Australian Dollar and 11.057% in Euros.
The spot exchange rate is AUD 1.7 per Euro.

                                                                                                                       23
 PROJECT AND PARENT PERSPECTIVES

Example:
Calculating the project’s perspective NPV:

 PROJECT AND PARENT PERSPECTIVES

Example: [OPTIONAL]
Note that the international parity conditions hold.
                                  1+𝑟
And since the real discount rates ( 1+𝑖 − 1) in both currencies are the same, the NPVs are the same in both
perspectives.


                                   ∗
                                           1 + 𝑟𝐴𝑈𝐷     1 + 10%
                                  𝑅𝐴𝑈𝐷 =            −1=         − 1 = 5.77%
                                           1 + 𝑖𝐴𝑈𝐷      1 + 4%


                                 ∗
                                         1 + 𝑟𝐸𝑈𝑅     1 + 11.057%
                                𝑅𝐸𝑈𝑅 =            −1=             − 1 = 5.77%
                                         1 + 𝑖𝐸𝑈𝑅        1 + 5%


                                                                                                                 27
 PROJECT AND PARENT PERSPECTIVES

Example 2:
You are considering an investment in Japan, which has a payoff of the following:
       Year                                   0            1              2            3         4         5
       Initial Investment (JPY)      -1,100,000
       FCFF (JPY)                                    300,000        300,000       300,000   300,000   300,000
       Expected Inflation in AUD            5%                 Discount rate in AUD                       8%
       Expected Inflation in JPY           20%                 Discount rate in JPY                      10%

The current spot exchange rate is JPY100 per AUD.
Estimate the NPV in terms of the project and the parent.




                                                                                                                   28
 PROJECT AND PARENT PERSPECTIVES

Example 2:
   Year                                 0         1            2            3         4         5
   Initial Investment (JPY)    -1,100,000
   FCFF (JPY)                               300,000      300,000       300,000   300,000   300,000
   Expected Inflation in AUD          5%            Discount rate in AUD                       8%
   Expected Inflation in JPY         20%            Discount rate in JPY                      10%
   PV (JPY)                    -1,100,000   272,727      247,934       225,394   204,904   186,276
   NPV (JPY)                       37,236           NPV(AUD)               372
   Exchang rate AUD/JPY              0.01
   E(Exchange rate AUD/JPY)        0.0100    0.0088       0.0077       0.0067     0.0059    0.0051
   E(FCFF, AUD)                   -11,000     2,625        2,297        2,010      1,759     1,539
   E(PV, AUD)                     -11,000     2,431        1,969        1,595      1,293     1,047
   NPV (AUD)                       -2,665
                                                                                                    29
 PROJECT AND PARENT PERSPECTIVES

Decision-making:




                                     30
 THE VALUE OF THE MULTINATIONAL FINANCIAL SYSTEM
 THE VALUE OF THE MULTINATIONAL FINANCIAL SYSTEM

The Advantage of Multi-National Corporations (MNC) Over Domestic Firms
MNCs, by virtue of having operations in more than one country, enjoy a fundamental advantage compared with firms
that only have operations in one country.
MNCs have the ability to move money and profits among company units through an intercompany fund-flow (internal
financial transfer) mechanisms. The key mechanisms are:



         Transfer Pricing                Inter-company Loans               Dividend Payments



       Leading & Lagging
                                        Fees/Royalty Charges              Reinvoicing Centres
           Payments
                                                                                                               32
 THE VALUE OF THE MULTINATIONAL FINANCIAL SYSTEM

The Advantage of Multi-National Corporations (MNC) Over Domestic Firms
Intercompany and intra-company fund flows become more valuable if the following situations arise:
     •   Taxation arbitrage
     •   Financial system arbitrage
     •   Regulatory system arbitrage


The extent to which MNCs can profit from cross country differences depends on how large the market imperfections are in
terms of the potential for tax arbitrage, financial system arbitrage and the regulatory system arbitrage.




                                                                                                                         33
 THE VALUE OF THE MULTINATIONAL FINANCIAL SYSTEM

TAX Advantages
•   A situation where there exists a high degree of cross-country variation in corporate tax systems and tax rates.
•   In other words, when tax systems across nations are highly segmented, there is room for arbitrage.


•   In general, a MNC’s global tax burden can be reduced by:
     •   Shifting profits from units in high-tax nations to those in lower-tax nations.
     •   Shifting profits from units in a tax paying position to those with tax losses.




                                                                                                                     34
      THE VALUE OF THE MULTINATIONAL FINANCIAL
                                        1980
                                                SYSTEM
                                             1990  2000 2010                                                              2020        2021
                                                                                  Australia           46   39   34   30    30          30

     TAX Advantages                                                               Brazil              35   30   37   34    34          34

rates-by-country-2022/                                                                                                              35

 THE VALUE OF THE MULTINATIONAL FINANCIAL SYSTEM

Tax Haven Countries




                                                     36
 THE VALUE OF THE MULTINATIONAL FINANCIAL SYSTEM

Tax Haven Countries




                                                     37
 THE VALUE OF THE MULTINATIONAL FINANCIAL SYSTEM

Tax Haven Countries




                                                     38
 THE VALUE OF THE MULTINATIONAL FINANCIAL SYSTEM
                        The G7 group of advanced economies has reached a "historic" deal to make
                        multinational companies pay more tax.
Tax Haven Countries
                        Finance ministers meeting in London agreed to battle tax avoidance by making companies
                        pay more in the countries where they do business.

                        They also agreed in principle to a global minimum corporate tax rate of 15% to avoid
                        countries undercutting each other.
                        Tech giants Amazon and Facebook are among those likely to be affected.

                        Why did they want to change the rules?
                        That challenge has grown with the boom in huge tech corporations like Amazon and
                        Facebook.

                        At the moment companies can set up local branches in countries that have relatively low
                        corporate tax rates and declare profits there.

                        That means they only pay the local rate of tax, even if the profits mainly come from sales
                        made elsewhere. This is legal and commonly done.


                                                                                                             39
 THE VALUE OF THE MULTINATIONAL FINANCIAL SYSTEM

Financial system advantage
•   Financial system arbitrage is possible if the international parity conditions do not hold.
•   In that case, firms can reduce their borrowing costs by borrowing in countries with lower interest rates, or they
    can earn higher returns by investing in countries with higher interest rates.


•   MNCs can also circumvent exchange controls, earn higher risk-adjusted yields on excess funds, and reduce cost of
    borrowing by transferring funds among units.


•   Exchange controls: government regulations restricting the transfer of funds to non-residents.




                                                                                                                       40
 THE VALUE OF THE MULTINATIONAL FINANCIAL SYSTEM

Regulatory system advantage
•   The profits of an MNC’s subsidiary may vary because of local regulations.


•   For example:
     •   Government’s price controls,
     •   Wage pressures by trade unions,
     •   Stringent labour market regulations.


•   MNCs may be able to disguise their true profitability by reallocating profits to other units => negotiating
    advantage (allowing them to circumvent strict regulations).

                                                                                                                     41
 THE VALUE OF THE MULTINATIONAL FINANCIAL SYSTEM

Channels
Channels commonly used to move money and profits internationally
    •   Transfer Pricing
    •   Reinvoicing Centers
    •   Fees and Royalties
    •   Leading and Lagging
    •   Dividends




                                                                      42
 THE VALUE OF THE MULTINATIONAL FINANCIAL SYSTEM

Example
•   Suppose Microsoft comprises two affiliate firms, XP and Vista. XP produces 80,000 circuit boards per annum for
    USD9 per unit and sells them to Vista. In turn, Vista sells these boards for USD20 per unit to the US government.
    Both affiliates have USD92,000 of overhead costs not related to the production of the circuit boards. XP has a
    marginal tax rate of 20%, while Vista has a marginal tax rate of 25%. XP can choose to sell the boards to Vista at a
    price of USD12 per unit, or at USD16 per unit.


•   How will XP’s choice of transfer price affect the profits of Microsoft?




                                                                                                                      43
 THE VALUE OF THE MULTINATIONAL FINANCIAL SYSTEM

Example
Set price at USD 12 per unit

   P/L Statement Line Item
                               XP Tax @ 20%   Vista Tax @ 25%   Combined P/L Statement

   Sales Revenue                 $960,000       $1,600,000           $2,560,000
   COGS                          $720,000        $960,000            $1,680,000
   Gross Profit                  $240,000        $640,000             $880,000
   Other Operating Expenses      $92,000         $92,000              $184,000
   EBIT                          $148,000        $548,000             $696,000
   Tax Expense                   $29,600         $137,000             $166,600
   Net Income                    $118,400        $411,000             $529,400
                                                                                            44
 THE VALUE OF THE MULTINATIONAL FINANCIAL SYSTEM

Example
Set price at USD 16 per unit

   P/L Statement Line Item
                               XP Tax @ 20%   Vista Tax @ 25%   Combined P/L Statement

   Sales Revenue                $1,280,000      $1,600,000           $2,880,000
                                                                                  Thus, by setting
   COGS                          $720,000       $1,280,000           $2,000,000   the higher
   Gross Profit                  $560,000        $320,000             $880,000    transfer price,
                                                                                  Microsoft is able
   Other Operating Expenses      $92,000         $92,000              $184,000
                                                                                  to obtain an
   EBIT                          $468,000        $228,000             $696,000    incremental net
   Tax Expense                   $93,600         $57,000              $150,600    income of
                                                                                  $16,000.
   Net Income                    $374,400        $171,000             $545,400
                                                                                                 45
 THE VALUE OF THE MULTINATIONAL FINANCIAL SYSTEM

Transfer Pricing
•   Consider a standard situation where affiliate A operates in Country A and sells goods to affiliate B, who operates in
    Country B.
•   Both countries have marginal tax rates of 𝑇𝐴 and 𝑇𝐵 respectively, where 𝑇𝐴≠𝑇𝐵.


•   The strategy of the MNC is to set a price for the goods sold such that profits are transferred from the country with
    the higher tax rate to the country with the lower tax rate.


•   Thus:
            If 𝑇𝐴>𝑇𝐵, then the MNC should set the transfer price as low as possible.
            If 𝑇𝐴<𝑇𝐵, then the MNC should set the transfer price as high as possible.


                                                                                                                           46
 THE VALUE OF THE MULTINATIONAL FINANCIAL SYSTEM

Transfer Pricing
•   What if there are tariffs involved? Vista must now pay ad-valorem tariffs 一 import duties that are set as a percentage of the
    value of the imported goods 一 at the rate of 7%.
•   XP has a marginal tax rate of 20%, while Vista has a marginal tax rate of 25%.
•   What transfer price should XP choose? A higher transfer price will increase the duties that Vista must pay.
•   Note that tariffs are tax-deductible.


•   The higher the ad-valorem tariff relative to the tax differential, the more likely it is that a low transfer price is desirable.
    Mathematically, to increase profits in the presence of ad-valorem tariffs:
                                         𝑳𝒐𝒘 𝑇𝑟𝑎𝑛𝑠𝑓𝑒𝑟 𝑃𝑟𝑖𝑐𝑒 𝑖𝑓: (𝑇𝐴 − 𝑇𝐵) < 𝑡𝑎𝑟𝑖𝑓𝑓 𝑟𝑎𝑡𝑒
                                         𝑯𝒊𝒈𝒉 𝑇𝑟𝑎𝑛𝑠𝑓𝑒𝑟 𝑃𝑟𝑖𝑐𝑒 𝑖𝑓: (𝑇𝐴 − 𝑇𝐵) > 𝑡𝑎𝑟𝑖𝑓𝑓 𝑟𝑎𝑡𝑒


                                                                                                                                          47
 THE VALUE OF THE MULTINATIONAL FINANCIAL SYSTEM

 THE VALUE OF THE MULTINATIONAL FINANCIAL SYSTEM

Transfer Pricing
•   Consider a standard situation where affiliate A operates in Country A and sells goods to affiliate B, who operates in
    Country B.
•   Affiliate B must now pay ad-valorem tariffs –import duties that are set as a percentage of the value of the imported
    goods.
•   A higher transfer price will increase the duties that Affiliate B must pay.
•   But tariffs are costs => tax –deductible!
•   The higher the ad-valorem tariff relative to the tax differential, the more likely it is that a lower transfer price is
    desirable.

 THE VALUE OF THE MULTINATIONAL FINANCIAL SYSTEM

Reinvoicing Centers
A re-invoicing centre is typically established by MNCs in low tax rate nations. There has been increased demand for reinvoicing
centres since the expansion of ecommerce in the early 2000s.
•   The centre takes title to all goods sold by firm units.
•   The centre pays the seller within the MNC. The centre is also paid by buyer within the MNC.
Advantages:
      Easier control on currency exposure
      Can use invoice currency other than local foreign currency.
Disadvantages
      Increased communications costs
      Suspicion of tax evasion by local governments

                                                                                                                                 52
 THE VALUE OF THE MULTINATIONAL FINANCIAL SYSTEM

Reinvoicing Centers




                                                     53
 THE VALUE OF THE MULTINATIONAL FINANCIAL SYSTEM

Fees and Royalty Charges
•   The parent company charges subsidiaries a fee or royalty.
      •    Usually for intangible assets, e.g., headquarters advice, allocated overhead, patents, trademarks.
      •    Do not have a market reference price, thus making it difficult to evaluate.
      •    Have the same tax and exchange control effects as physical goods.
•   Imposes an expense on the subsidiary and gives revenue to the parent:
      •    Reduces taxable income,
      •    Enable fund flows between units.
•   The parent company has a control on a desired amount of total fee remittances from the overseas operations.
      •    However, the pricing of intangibles is carefully scrutinized by foreign country regulators.



                                                                                                                     54
 THE VALUE OF THE MULTINATIONAL FINANCIAL SYSTEM

Leading and Lagging
•   MNCs can shift cash (a measure of liquidity) among affiliates by accelerating (leading) or delaying (lagging) in the
    payment of inter-affiliate accounts by modifying the terms of credit.
•   Leading and lagging is a substitute for the MNC obtaining external (debt) financing.
•   By modifying the credit terms between two company units, funds can be shifted from units with a funding surplus to
    units with a deficit.
•   The process is highly favoured by MNCs.
      •    The value of leading and lagging depends on the opportunity cost of funds (i.e., whether a unit has a surplus
           (+) or deficit (–) of funds.
•   Opportunity Cost: the rates at which a unit with a deficit could borrow and a unit with a surplus could lend, calculated
    on an after-tax basis.



                                                                                                                          55
 THE VALUE OF THE MULTINATIONAL FINANCIAL SYSTEM

Leading and Lagging
Example: Suppose we have Affiliate X and Affiliate Y of a MNC. Affiliate X sells goods totalling USD350,000 per month to
Affiliate Y with trade credit terms of 90 days.
What happens to the cash of both affiliates with leading and lagging?


               0                30                60                90               120                180

                                                                1,050,000                            1,050,000
                                                               (3*350,000)                          (3*350,000)

                             350,000           350,000           350,000           350,000            350,000

                                                                                                     2,100,000
                                                                                                    (6*350,000)
                                                                                                                      56
 THE VALUE OF THE MULTINATIONAL FINANCIAL SYSTEM

Leading and Lagging
•   The value of leading and lagging ultimately depends on the opportunity cost of funds to both the paying unit and the
    recipient of funds.
•   The value depends on whether an affiliate has either surplus or deficit of funds.
•   Opportunity cost: borrowing (for the unit with deficit) and lending (for the unit with a surplus) rates are calculated on
    an after-tax basis.
•   The benefits of leading and lagging compared with direct inter-company loans are:
      •    No formal note of indebtedness is required,
      •    The amount of credit can be adjusted with a high degree of flexibility.




                                                                                                                               57
 THE VALUE OF THE MULTINATIONAL FINANCIAL SYSTEM

Leading and Lagging
Example: Suppose we have Affiliate X in the US and Affiliate Y in the UK of a MNC. Affiliate X sells goods totalling
USD350,000 per month to Affiliate Y with trade credit terms of 90 days. The borrowing and lending rates in the US are
5% and 6%, and the borrowing and lending rates in the UK are 5.2% and 6.2%,
What happens to the cash of both affiliates with leading and lagging?
•   Transferring money to the higher opportunity cost region
     •   Say, if Y needs to borrow from banks at the rate of 6.2% and X has some excess cash, by transferring 350k
         early (90days), it generates benefit of USD 350,000 * (6.2%-5%)*90/360 = USD 1,050.
     •   Say, if X needs to borrow from banks at the rate of 6% and Y has some excess cash, by transferring 350k early
         (90days), it generates benefit of USD 350,000 * (6%-5.2%)*90/360 = USD 700.




                                                                                                                        58
 THE VALUE OF THE MULTINATIONAL FINANCIAL SYSTEM

Dividend Policy
The most important means of transferring funds from foreign affiliates to the parent company (about 50% of all
remittances to U.S. firms).
•   The parent company’s dividend payout ratio is important. An equal percentage for each affiliate can be applied.
•   The MNC can minimize its total tax expense by varying payout ratios among its foreign subsidiaries.
•   Can shift liquidity among the various units; set a high(low) dividend payout rate for subsidiaries with relatively low
    (high) opportunity cost of funds.




                                                                                                                            59
